description: Canonical lesson schema prototypes. Only free-section material belongs in this public-build source until a premium-safe emitter is added.

0.mission: Run one Luau instruction, observe its result, and explain where that result appears.

mentalModel.title: Instructions become visible evidence
body: A script is a set of instructions. Luau runs those instructions in order; print is a simple way to make one result visible while you learn.

In Roblox Studio, scripts live in the DataModel. In LuauX Practice, the browser sandbox gives you a safe place to test language ideas. It is useful for learning Luau, but it is not a full Roblox engine simulation.

build.title: Send your first signal
steps: Open Practice and run the code exactly once.
Change only the text between the quotation marks, then run it again.
Say out loud what changed: the instruction stayed the same; the string value changed.

verify: The output contains LuauX online exactly once.
After changing the string, the output changes without changing the print instruction.
You can point to message as a name and "LuauX online" as a string value.

debug.title: A name is not text until you quote it
debug.symptom: The sandbox reports that it does not know what LuauX is.
debug.diagnosis: Without quotation marks, Luau reads LuauX and online as names it should resolve. Put text inside quotation marks so it is a string.

0.explain: local creates a local binding named message. print receives the value stored in that binding and writes a representation to the output. The same pattern—store state, pass it to a function, observe the result—will recur throughout the course.
0.extend: In Studio, create a Script in ServerScriptService, open Output, then run a playtest. Compare that Studio output with the browser sandbox: both teach you to observe code, but only Studio runs against a Roblox place.

1.mission: Read a short Luau instruction accurately enough to predict what is a name, a value, and punctuation.

mentalModel.title: Code is structured text
body: Luau reads characters using rules. Names identify values or functions; punctuation groups an instruction; quoted characters form a string.
You do not need compiler internals to debug a first script. Start by checking spelling, quotation marks, parentheses, and the exact name you intended to use.

build.title: Label a print instruction
steps: Run the code.
Change only one token at a time: the name, the punctuation, or the string.
Write down which change created a syntax error and which created a name error.

verify: The output is Welcome, Ari.
You can identify playerName as a name and "Welcome, " as a string.

debug.title: Capital letters change a name
debug.diagnosis: Luau names are case-sensitive. PlayerName and playerName are different names.

1.explain: Code is not guessed from intent. The parser needs the punctuation to be in the right places, and name lookup needs the spelling and capitalization to match the binding you created.
1.extend: Open the Luau grammar after you can reliably read small instructions. Treat it as a reference, not the first way to learn to script.

2.mission: Store and update local game state without leaking a name outside the scope where it belongs.

mentalModel.title: A local name has a home
body: A variable binding gives a value a name. local keeps that name in the block where you declared it, which makes dependencies easier to see and prevents accidental shared state.
A value can change while its name stays useful. What matters is which binding your code can currently see.

build.title: Track one coin pickup
steps: Run the code, then change the starting number and predict the final output before running again.
Add another local named pickupAmount and use it instead of the literal 5.
Keep both names local; do not create a global just to share a value.
build.code:
local coins = 25
local pickupAmount = 5

coins += pickupAmount
print("Coins: " .. coins)

verify: The initial values 25 and 5 print Coins: 30.
Changing pickupAmount changes the result predictably.
The code uses local for both pieces of state.

debug.title: A branch-local name cannot escape the branch
debug.symptom: The second print cannot find bonus.
debug.code:
if true then
    local bonus = 10
    print(bonus)
end

print(bonus)
debug.diagnosis: bonus exists only inside the if block. If later code needs a value, declare the outer binding before the branch and assign it inside the branch.

2.explain: The assignment operator updates the existing coins binding. A block can read names from an outer scope, but a local declared inside the block is not available after end. This is a readability and correctness tool first—not a reason to chase micro-optimizations.
2.extend: Turn the pickup into a function that receives the current count and returns the next count. That prepares you for the state-and-function patterns in the next units.

3.mission: Combine values in the intended order and make an expression readable enough to verify.

mentalModel.title: An expression produces one value
body: Operators combine values. Parentheses make the order explicit when a human reader could reasonably misunderstand it.
Use numbers for arithmetic and .. for string concatenation. A quoted number is text, not a number you can safely add.

build.title: Calculate a round reward
steps: Run the reward calculation.
Change multiplier and bonus separately and predict the result.
Add parentheses around the multiplication so the intended order is visible.
build.code:
local baseReward = 20
local multiplier = 2
local bonus = 5
local reward = (baseReward * multiplier) + bonus
print("Reward: " .. reward)

verify: The initial output is Reward: 45.
Changing bonus does not change the multiplication result.

debug.title: Text cannot do arithmetic
debug.diagnosis: Quotation marks make 20 a string. Keep arithmetic values as numbers; convert to text only when presenting output.

3.explain: The parentheses calculate baseReward times multiplier first. The resulting number is then added to bonus. Concatenation happens only when the final number is presented beside text.
3.extend: Use a conditional expression to clamp a reward to a minimum, then compare a readable two-step calculation with a dense one-line expression.

4.mission: Write a clear gameplay gate using Luau truthiness and short-circuit logic.

mentalModel.title: Only false and nil stop the gate
body: In Luau, only false and nil are falsy. Zero, an empty string, and an empty table are still truthy, so do not use them as if they mean no.
and and or evaluate left to right and may stop early. They return one of their operand values, not necessarily the boolean words true or false.

build.title: Gate a locked door
steps: Run the code with hasKey set to false, then set it to true.
Add a second flag called isDoorPowered and require both conditions.
Test with zero and explain why zero does not behave like false in Luau.
build.code:
local hasKey = false
local isDoorPowered = true
local canOpen = hasKey and isDoorPowered

if canOpen then
    print("Door opened")
else
    print("Find the key first")
end

verify: false and true prints Find the key first.
true and true prints Door opened.
You can explain why 0 is not a substitute for false.

debug.title: The old and-or shortcut loses false
debug.symptom: The expression returns the fallback even when hasAccess is true.
debug.code: local isHidden = hasAccess and false or true
debug.diagnosis: When hasAccess is true, the and expression produces false, so or continues to true. Use a Luau if expression when either intended result can be false or nil.

4.explain: Short-circuiting is useful because the right side is not evaluated when the left side already determines the result. Use it for readable guards, but use an if expression when you are choosing between values and either result may be falsy.
4.extend: Rewrite the door state as an if expression: local message = if canOpen then "Door opened" else "Find the key first". Compare it with the statement form.

5.mission: Choose one gameplay outcome with an if statement, then use an if expression when a value is required.

mentalModel.title: A conditional chooses a path
body: An if statement chooses which block of instructions runs. Each branch has its own local scope, so temporary work stays close to the decision that needs it.
An if expression chooses a value. It requires an else because it must always produce one result.

build.title: Classify a player state
steps: Run the code with health set to 40, then 0, then -5.
Add an elseif branch for low health below 20.
Use the returned status in a second print so you can see the value separately from the decision.
build.code:
local health = 40

local status = if health <= 0 then "Respawning" else "Active"

if health <= 0 then
    print("Disable player input")
else
    print("Keep playing")
end

print("Status: " .. status)

verify: health 40 prints Keep playing and Status: Active.
health 0 prints Disable player input and Status: Respawning.
Your elseif branch has a condition and the whole statement still ends once.

debug.title: elseif is one word
debug.symptom: The parser asks for another end or the branches nest in an unexpected way.
debug.code:
if health <= 0 then
    print("Respawning")
else if health < 20 then
    print("Low health")
end
debug.diagnosis: else if starts a new if inside else, so it needs another end. Use elseif for one decision chain, or deliberately add the second end for a nested decision.

5.explain: Statements control which instructions happen. Expressions produce a value that can be assigned. Keeping temporary locals inside the branch makes it clear which outcome owns them and prevents later code from depending on a value that was never created.
5.extend: Create a three-state status with an if expression: Respawning, Low health, or Active. Then decide which part should remain a statement because it performs an action rather than calculates a value.

6.mission: Use a bounded loop to repeat a deliberate task without creating an accidental infinite loop.

mentalModel.title: A loop needs a reason to stop
body: A loop repeats a block. A numeric for loop is ideal when you know the number of repetitions; a while loop is for a condition that can change.
Before running a loop, state what changes and what eventually stops it.

build.title: Spawn three practice rewards
steps: Run the numeric loop.
Change the upper bound from 3 to 5 and predict the number of lines.
Add the loop index to the printed message.
build.code:
for rewardNumber = 1, 3 do
    print("Spawn reward #" .. rewardNumber)
end

verify: The output has exactly three lines at the initial bound.
The final line includes reward #3.

debug.title: A while loop must change its condition
debug.symptom: The script keeps printing and never reaches the next line.
debug.code:
local count = 0
while count < 3 do
    print(count)
end
debug.diagnosis: count never changes, so count < 3 remains true. Increment count inside the loop or use a numeric for loop when the bounds are known.

6.explain: The numeric for loop owns its counter and endpoint. This makes the stop condition visible in one place and avoids a common class of accidental endless work.
6.extend: Use continue to skip one loop iteration, then decide whether a filtering condition would be clearer before the loop instead.

7.mission: Walk through a collection and use the value you actually need.

mentalModel.title: Collections have positions and values
body: An array stores ordered values. A for-in loop can give you both an index and a value, so choose names that reveal which one you use.
Iteration is not magic: each turn of the loop binds the next result before running the body.

build.title: Read a pickup list
steps: Run the loop.
Add a fourth pickup.
Change the print to include both index and pickup.
build.code:
local pickups = {"Coin", "Gem", "Key"}

for index, pickup in pickups do
    print(index .. ": " .. pickup)
end

verify: Each pickup appears once in the output.
The index and the pickup are not confused.

debug.title: Use the value, not the numeric position
debug.symptom: The UI prints 1, 2, 3 instead of the item names.
debug.code:
for index, pickup in pickups do
    print(index)
end
debug.diagnosis: index is the position. pickup is the value stored at that position. Choose the binding that matches what the output needs.

7.explain: The loop creates fresh local bindings for each result. Naming them index and pickup makes the data shape visible and reduces accidental use of the wrong variable.
7.extend: Compare an ordered array with a dictionary of named settings, then choose an iteration shape that communicates the data you own.

8.mission: Choose direct generalized iteration for a table before reaching for older iterator helpers.

mentalModel.title: The collection can provide the next result
body: Modern Luau supports generalized iteration, so a table can be used directly in a for-in loop.
The point is not memorizing a clever shorthand. The point is to make it obvious what collection you are traversing and what each loop receives.

build.title: Inspect named settings
steps: Run the loop.
Add one setting and predict its key/value output.
Replace the names with a game system you recognize.
build.code:
local settings = {music = true, volume = 0.7, difficulty = "normal"}

for key, value in settings do
    print(key .. ": " .. tostring(value))
end

verify: Every named setting prints a key and a value.
You use tostring only to present non-string values in text.

debug.title: A table value may not concatenate as text
debug.diagnosis: settings.volume is a number. Convert it deliberately for output with tostring, while keeping it numeric for calculations.

8.explain: Generalized iteration makes the table the source of results. The key/value names are local to each loop turn, so they should describe the shape of the collection rather than the implementation detail.
8.extend: Read the generalized-iteration syntax notes, then compare it with a numeric loop over an array and explain which one preserves the intended order.

9.mission: Write a small function that receives state, returns the next state, and makes its effect easy to test.

mentalModel.title: A function is a named contract
body: A function names a repeatable piece of work. Parameters are the inputs it receives; return values are the results it sends back.
Prefer returning the next value instead of silently changing distant state. It makes a function easier to test, reuse, and move into a ModuleScript later.

build.title: Award a coin pickup
steps: Run the code and predict the output before you do.
Call awardCoins with a different pickup amount.
Add a guard so a negative pickup does not reduce coins.
build.code:
local function awardCoins(currentCoins: number, pickupAmount: number): number
    return currentCoins + pickupAmount
end

local coins = 25
coins = awardCoins(coins, 5)
print("Coins: " .. coins)

verify: The initial call prints Coins: 30.
Changing the second argument changes only the amount awarded.
You assign the returned value back into coins.

debug.title: Returning a value does not update a caller by itself
debug.symptom: The function runs, but coins still prints 25.
debug.diagnosis: awardCoins returns a new number. Store that return value: coins = awardCoins(coins, 5).

9.explain: Function parameters are local bindings for one call. Numbers are values, so assigning a new number inside the function does not change the caller's binding. Returning the next value makes the state transition explicit. The annotations help the type checker describe the intended inputs and result; they do not convert values at runtime.
9.extend: Make a table-based player state and return a new result deliberately. Then compare dot and colon method syntax only after you understand the explicit parameter form.

10.mission: Use an outer local deliberately inside a function and recognize when a closure keeps that state available.

mentalModel.title: A function can remember an outer binding
body: A function declared inside another scope can read a local from the outer scope. That remembered relationship is called a closure.
This is useful for small, owned state. Do not use it to hide state that another part of the game must understand or validate.

build.title: Make a local score counter
steps: Run the counter twice.
Change the increment.
Explain which local stays available between calls.
build.code:
local score = 0

local function addScore(amount)
    score += amount
    return score
end

print(addScore(10))
print(addScore(5))

verify: The outputs are 10 then 15.
You can identify score as the outer state used by addScore.

debug.title: A new local resets the state
debug.symptom: Every call returns the same small value.
debug.code:
local function addScore(amount)
    local score = 0
    score += amount
    return score
end
debug.diagnosis: score is recreated for every call. Put the owned state in the intended outer scope, or pass it in and return the next value when shared state should stay explicit.

10.explain: The function closes over the outer score binding. That gives the counter memory between calls, but it also creates a dependency you should name and keep small.
10.extend: Refactor the counter to accept current score and return next score. Compare explicit state with closure-owned state and choose based on who needs to own the truth.

11.mission: Choose a loop for repeated gameplay work and recognize recursion as a controlled, smaller-problem technique.

mentalModel.title: Every call needs a return path
body: A function call creates work that must finish before the caller continues. Recursion is a function calling itself with a smaller problem.
In game scripts, a loop is usually clearer for repeating over a known collection. Use recursion only when the structure itself is recursive and you can state the base case.

build.title: Count down with a base case
steps: Run the function with 3.
Change the base case to stop at 1.
Explain which call prevents the next call.
build.code:
local function countdown(value)
    if value <= 0 then
        print("Go!")
        return
    end

    print(value)
    countdown(value - 1)
end

countdown(3)

verify: The output is 3, 2, 1, Go!.
You can point to the base case and the smaller next call.

debug.title: A recursive call must move toward its base case
debug.diagnosis: Passing the same value repeats the same problem. Reduce the argument or choose a loop if the work is simply a known number of repetitions.

11.explain: The base case returns without another call. Each other call receives a smaller value, so the chain eventually reaches that return path.
11.extend: Trace a nested table search on paper before coding it. If the structure is flat, prefer a loop and explain why it is easier to inspect.

sources.label: Luau grammar: function calls and conditionals

12.mission: Create an ordered table, update one item, and avoid assuming the length operator proves a table has no gaps.

mentalModel.title: An array is an ordered table convention
body: Luau tables can hold values at numeric keys. When you use consecutive numeric positions starting at 1, you are using the common array convention.
The length operator is useful for dense arrays, but a table with missing numeric positions is not a reliable place to guess from # alone.

build.title: Equip a three-slot inventory
steps: Run the code.
Replace the second item.
Append one item with table.insert.
build.code:
local inventory = {"Sword", "Potion", "Key"}
inventory[2] = "Shield"
table.insert(inventory, "Map")

print(inventory[2])
print(#inventory)

verify: The second item is Shield.
The dense inventory length is 4 after insertion.

debug.title: Tables start their normal array positions at 1
debug.symptom: The first inventory lookup returns nil.
debug.diagnosis: The array convention uses 1 for the first element. Use inventory[1] for the first item and keep ordered numeric keys dense.

12.explain: A table is flexible; the array behavior comes from the keys you choose. Keeping inventory entries consecutive makes indexing, insertion, and length checks predictable.
12.extend: Create a sparse table intentionally and observe why # is no longer a trustworthy count. Then decide whether a dictionary key would communicate the model better.

13.mission: Use named table keys for state where a position would hide the meaning.

mentalModel.title: A dictionary answers by name
body: A dictionary-style table maps meaningful keys to values. Use it for named settings, player state, or lookup data where position is not the important fact.
Reading a missing key produces nil, so guard optional data before using it as if it exists.

build.title: Read a player state record
steps: Run the code.
Add a level field.
Use a conditional to handle a missing title safely.

verify: coins prints 30 and title prints Rookie.
A missing key is checked before concatenation or property-style use.

debug.title: A missing key is nil
debug.symptom: Concatenation fails after reading a title that was never set.
debug.diagnosis: rank is nil because the table has no rank key. Supply a fallback after checking the value, or ensure the state record creates the key.

13.explain: Dot syntax is a readable form for string-like keys. The important decision is semantic: coins communicates a fact directly, while playerState[1] would force every reader to remember an unwritten convention.
13.extend: Nest a small settings table, then decide where a separate ModuleScript becomes clearer than one growing record.

14.mission: Use math helpers to keep a gameplay value inside an intentional range.

mentalModel.title: A range is a rule you can enforce
body: Gameplay values often need boundaries: health should not drop below zero, volume should stay between zero and one, and a random reward should come from a stated range.
math.clamp expresses a boundary directly. It is clearer than scattering separate lower and upper checks through a system.

build.title: Clamp player health
steps: Run the code with health below zero.
Test a health value above maxHealth.
Change maxHealth and predict the result.
build.code:
local health = -15
local maxHealth = 100
health = math.clamp(health, 0, maxHealth)
print("Health: " .. health)

verify: -15 becomes 0.
A value above maxHealth becomes maxHealth.

debug.title: Clamp arguments have an order
debug.symptom: The result does not use the value you expected.
debug.diagnosis: math.clamp takes value, minimum, maximum. Put the state you are constraining first, then its allowed range.

14.explain: The function returns a value inside the inclusive minimum/maximum range. Assigning that result back to health makes the state rule explicit at the point it changes.
14.extend: Use math.random for a clearly bounded practice reward, then separate randomness from any paid or fairness-sensitive game economy design.

15.mission: Use a table helper to update a collection intentionally and verify the collection before and after.

mentalModel.title: A helper changes one collection rule
body: The table library provides operations such as insert, remove, clear, and clone. Use the helper that states the change you want instead of rebuilding a collection through accidental side effects.
When a collection represents game state, check the before and after values so a removal or insertion is observable.

build.title: Consume one inventory item
steps: Run the code.
Remove a different position.
Print the remaining items with a loop.
build.code:
local inventory = {"Potion", "Key", "Map"}
local usedItem = table.remove(inventory, 1)

print("Used: " .. usedItem)
print("Remaining: " .. #inventory)

verify: usedItem is Potion.
The dense inventory has two remaining items.

debug.title: Removing changes later numeric positions
debug.symptom: The item you expected at index 2 is now somewhere else.
debug.diagnosis: Dense arrays shift later items left after a removal. Re-read the collection or use named keys when identity matters more than position.

15.explain: table.remove returns the removed value and updates the dense numeric sequence. Capturing that return makes the consumed item explicit instead of losing it during the mutation.
15.extend: Use table.clone to compare a draft collection with an original, then verify what is and is not copied before treating it as an independent deep state copy.
